formulation: The progressive claim — that expanding rights to women, Black people, and LGBTQ+ citizens fulfilled the West's founding creed — is the only zero-to-one argument in this debate: it names a genuinely new thing rather than a return to a prior state. The conservative-restoration case is mimetic by structure, defining the good as what was lost and seeking to recapture it. If the test of civilizational vitality is the capacity to produce the genuinely new rather than to fight over the old, the progressive reading has the stronger claim.
context_narrative: At the Munich Security Conference, a heated panel cracked open over a question no one could settle: who owns 'the West'? Gladden Pappin and Petr Macinka argued that progressive overreach — cancel culture, gender ideology, the erosion of family and borders — had hollowed out Western civilization's strategic foundations; Hillary Clinton fired back that expanding rights to Black people, women, and LGBTQ+ citizens was the fulfillment of the West's founding creed, not its betrayal. Ivan Krastev cut sideways, pointing out that 'we have too many Wests' to anchor either claim, and used Denmark and Greenland to show that civilizational rhetoric collapses the moment it meets its own logic. The room never resolved whether the conservative-progressive frame was even the right one — or whether Trump represents conservatism at all.

selected_quotes.quote: The West is no longer a unified civilization but a contested territory of values, divided between a conservative vision rooted in national sovereignty and tradition, and a progressive vision of supranational social engineering, with the gap widening.
The expansion of freedom to include Black people, women, and gay people — including same-sex marriage — was a culmination of Western values, not a progressive distortion of them.

Conservatism has changed because both center-left social progressivism and center-right free-market orthodoxy didn't know where to stop, creating a bipartisan 'conspiracy of the center' that sold out the country in the 1990s and 2000s.
selected_quotes.flavor: responding to the previous speaker, with intensity

selected_quotes.quote: Being highly nationalistic triggers the nationalism of others, which is why far-right parties in Europe strongly opposed Trump on Greenland — for them, land is not real estate — exposing a major contradiction in the conservative-nationalist position.

rationale: This proposition weaponizes Thiel's own zero-to-one and Girardian frameworks against his likely political sympathies, forcing him to either concede the progressive narrative is structurally more innovative than the conservative one, argue that rights expansion is itself mimetic rather than genuinely new, or abandon the zero-to-one lens for civilizational questions — landing squarely in his activates_on territory while pressing hard into his stretch.
